title: Listening - Part 4
description: For each question, choose the correct answer.
instructions: You will hear an interview. For questions 20–25, choose the answer (A, B or C) which fits best according to what you hear.
context: You will hear an interview with a woman called Olivia Reed, who works as a museum guide.
audio_script: Interviewer: Today I'm speaking to Olivia Reed, who works as a guide in a large city museum. Olivia, what made you want to work in a museum? || Guest: I loved history when I was younger, but I didn't imagine working with it. I wanted to be a teacher. During university, I had a part-time job selling tickets at a museum. [20]I enjoyed watching visitors become interested in objects they had never heard of before[/20], and I realised that museums could teach people in a very lively way. || Interviewer: What do you do before you lead a tour? || Guest: I read the information, of course, but that isn't enough. [21]I always try to choose a few stories that connect the objects with ordinary people's lives[/21]. Dates and names matter, but visitors remember a story about a family, a journey or a mistake much more easily. || Interviewer: Are school groups difficult to guide? || Guest: They can be noisy, but I enjoy them. Children ask direct questions, which is refreshing. [22]The hardest groups are adults who arrive thinking museums are boring[/22]. You can see it in their faces. If I can make them smile after ten minutes, I feel I've done my job. || Interviewer: Has technology changed your work? || Guest: Yes, but not completely. Audio guides and screens are useful, especially for people who want to move at their own speed. However, [23]visitors still tell us they value having a real person to ask questions[/23]. A guide can change the tour depending on the group, and a screen can't do that. || Interviewer: What is your favourite object in the museum? || Guest: It changes, but at the moment it's a small wooden toy horse. It isn't beautiful or expensive, and many people walk past it. [24]I like it because it reminds visitors that children in the past had fun just like children today[/24]. It makes history feel close. || Interviewer: What is coming soon at the museum? || Guest: We're opening a new exhibition about travel next month. Some people expect only famous explorers, but [25]we're also including stories from ordinary people who moved to the city for work[/25]. I think visitors will recognise parts of their own family history in it. || Interviewer: Do you ever get tired of giving the same tour? || Guest: Not really, because every group notices different things. One visitor may ask about clothes, another about food, and another about how people travelled. Their questions make the tour feel new. I also change small details depending on the age of the group and the time we have. || Interviewer: What advice would you give someone visiting a museum? || Guest: Do not try to see everything. Choose one room, slow down, and look properly at a few objects. Read one label carefully, then ask yourself who used the object and why it mattered. That is much better than rushing past hundreds of things and remembering nothing afterwards. || Interviewer: What would you change about museums if you could? || Guest: I would make the entrances feel more welcoming. Some people think museums are only for experts, and that is a shame. A friendly welcome, clear signs and simple information can make a nervous visitor feel that the place belongs to them too.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Today I'm speaking to Olivia Reed, who works as a guide in a large city museum. Olivia, what made you want to work in a museum?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I loved history when I was younger, but I didn't imagine working with it. I wanted to be a teacher. During university, I had a part-time job selling tickets at a museum. I enjoyed watching visitors become interested in objects they had never heard of before, and I realised that museums could teach people in a very lively way.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What do you do before you lead a tour?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I read the information, of course, but that isn't enough. I always try to choose a few stories that connect the objects with ordinary people's lives. Dates and names matter, but visitors remember a story about a family, a journey or a mistake much more easily.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Are school groups difficult to guide?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: They can be noisy, but I enjoy them. Children ask direct questions, which is refreshing. The hardest groups are adults who arrive thinking museums are boring. You can see it in their faces. If I can make them smile after ten minutes, I feel I've done my job.

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Yes, but not completely. Audio guides and screens are useful, especially for people who want to move at their own speed. However, visitors still tell us they value having a real person to ask questions. A guide can change the tour depending on the group, and a screen can't do that.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What is your favourite object in the museum?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: It changes, but at the moment it's a small wooden toy horse. It isn't beautiful or expensive, and many people walk past it. I like it because it reminds visitors that children in the past had fun just like children today. It makes history feel close.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What is coming soon at the museum?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: We're opening a new exhibition about travel next month. Some people expect only famous explorers, but we're also including stories from ordinary people who moved to the city for work. I think visitors will recognise parts of their own family history in it.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Do you ever get tired of giving the same tour?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Not really, because every group notices different things. One visitor may ask about clothes, another about food, and another about how people travelled. Their questions make the tour feel new. I also change small details depending on the age of the group and the time we have.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What advice would you give someone visiting a museum?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Do not try to see everything. Choose one room, slow down, and look properly at a few objects. Read one label carefully, then ask yourself who used the object and why it mattered. That is much better than rushing past hundreds of things and remembering nothing afterwards.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What would you change about museums if you could?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I would make the entrances feel more welcoming. Some people think museums are only for experts, and that is a shame. A friendly welcome, clear signs and simple information can make a nervous visitor feel that the place belongs to them too.

questions.question: Why did Olivia become interested in museum work?

options.A: She was asked to teach history classes there.
options.B: She wanted to continue studying at university.
options.C: She enjoyed seeing visitors discover new things.

questions.explanation: Olivia says she enjoyed watching visitors become interested in objects they had never heard of before.

questions.question: How does Olivia prepare for a tour?

options.A: She learns all the dates carefully.
options.B: She selects stories people can relate to.

questions.explanation: Olivia says she chooses stories that connect the objects with ordinary people's lives.

questions.question: Which visitors does Olivia find most difficult?

options.C: Adults who expect not to enjoy the museum.

questions.explanation: Olivia says the hardest groups are adults who arrive thinking museums are boring.

questions.question: What does Olivia say about technology in museums?

options.A: It should replace most guided tours.
options.B: It helps people move more quickly.
options.C: It cannot answer visitors' questions like a guide.

questions.explanation: Olivia says visitors value having a real person to ask questions.

questions.question: Why does Olivia like the wooden toy horse?

options.A: It shows that children in the past were similar to children now.
options.B: It is one of the most valuable objects in the museum.
options.C: It was made by a famous person from the city.

questions.explanation: Olivia says the toy reminds visitors that children in the past had fun like children today.

questions.question: What is special about the new exhibition?

options.A: It focuses only on famous explorers.
options.B: It includes stories of ordinary people.
options.C: It shows objects from foreign museums.

questions.explanation: Olivia says the museum is including stories from ordinary people who moved to the city for work.
